healthy.cause: No disease symptoms detected. The plant appears healthy.
healthy.prevention: Continue regular crop monitoring, balanced fertilization, and proper watering.

default.cause: Pathogen infection (fungal, bacterial, or viral) or environmental stress.
default.cure: Remove heavily affected leaves. Apply a broad-spectrum fungicide or bactericide as appropriate.
default.prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds, rotate crops, maintain field hygiene, avoid overhead watering.

Apple scab.cause: Caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. Thrives in cool, wet spring weather.
Apple scab.cure: Apply fungicides such as captan or myclobutanil. Remove and destroy infected leaves and fruit.
Apple scab.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, ensure good air circulation, apply dormant sprays before bud break.

Apple Cedar apple rust.cause: Caused by the fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. Requires both apple and juniper hosts.
Apple Cedar apple rust.cure: Apply fungicides (myclobutanil, propiconazole) at early infection stages. Remove galls on nearby junipers.
Apple Cedar apple rust.prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties, remove juniper hosts within 500m, apply preventive fungicides.

Apple Black rot.cause: Caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. Spreads through infected bark and mummified fruit.
Apple Black rot.cure: Prune infected cankers 15cm below diseased tissue. Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl fungicides.
Apple Black rot.prevention: Maintain good sanitation, remove mummified fruit, prune dead wood, ensure proper nutrition.

Tomato Early blight.cause: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Appears as dark concentric rings (target-board pattern) on lower leaves. Favored by warm, humid conditions.
Tomato Early blight.cure: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper-based products. Remove severely infected lower leaves immediately.
Tomato Early blight.prevention: Use disease-resistant varieties, practice crop rotation (3-year minimum), stake plants for better airflow, avoid wetting foliage.

Tomato Late blight.cause: Caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Causes large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions on leaves and stems. Spreads rapidly in cool, wet conditions.
Tomato Late blight.cure: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or metalaxyl immediately. Remove and destroy all infected plant tissue.
Tomato Late blight.prevention: Plant certified disease-free transplants, avoid overhead irrigation, ensure good air circulation, apply preventive copper sprays.

Tomato Leaf Mold.cause: Caused by the fungus Passalora fulva (syn. Cladosporium fulvum). Yellow patches on upper leaf surface with olive-green mold on underside. Thrives in high humidity (>85%).
Tomato Leaf Mold.cure: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper hydroxide). Improve greenhouse ventilation and reduce humidity.
Tomato Leaf Mold.prevention: Use resistant varieties, maintain humidity below 85%, ensure good air circulation, avoid wetting leaves.

Tomato Septoria leaf spot.cause: Caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. Shows as small circular spots with grayish centers and dark borders with yellow halos on lower leaves.
Tomato Septoria leaf spot.cure: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper sprays) at first sign of infection. Remove infected lower leaves.
Tomato Septoria leaf spot.prevention: Rotate crops for 2+ years, use mulch to prevent soil splash, stake plants, use drip irrigation.

Tomato Spider mites Two spotted spider m.cause: Infestation by Tetranychus urticae. Causes stippling, yellowing, and bronzing of leaves. Mites form fine webbing on leaf undersides.
Tomato Spider mites Two spotted spider m.cure: Apply miticides (abamectin, bifenazate) or insecticidal soap/neem oil. Remove heavily infested leaves.
Tomato Spider mites Two spotted spider m.prevention: Maintain plant vigor with proper watering, encourage natural predators (predatory mites), avoid dusty conditions.

Tomato Target Spot.cause: Caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola. Circular lesions with concentric rings and yellow halos on leaves, stems, and fruit.
Tomato Target Spot.cure: Apply fungicides (azoxystrobin, chlorothalonil). Remove and destroy severely infected plant material.
Tomato Target Spot.prevention: Use resistant varieties, practice crop rotation, improve airflow, avoid overhead irrigation.

Tomato Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.cause: Caused by Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) transmitted by whiteflies (Bemisia tabaci). Symptoms: upward leaf curling, yellowing, stunting.
Tomato Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.cure: No cure once infected. Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread. Control whitefly populations with insecticides.
Tomato Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.prevention: Use TYLCV-resistant varieties, install yellow sticky traps, use reflective mulches, apply insecticides to control whiteflies.

Tomato Tomato mosaic virus.cause: Caused by Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV), transmitted mechanically via tools, hands, or infected soil. Shows as mosaic leaf patterns, distortion, and stunted growth.
Tomato Tomato mosaic virus.cure: No chemical cure. Remove and destroy infected plants. Sterilize tools with 10% bleach solution.
Tomato Tomato mosaic virus.prevention: Use resistant varieties, wash hands before handling plants, sterilize tools, avoid tobacco products near plants.

Tomato Bacterial spot.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria bacteria. Water-soaked spots that turn dark brown with yellow halos on leaves and fruit.
Tomato Bacterial spot.cure: Apply copper-based bactericides combined with mancozeb at first signs. Remove and destroy heavily infected plant tissue.
Tomato Bacterial spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed, practice crop rotation, avoid overhead irrigation, use resistant varieties.

Potato Early blight.cause: Caused by Alternaria solani fungus. Dark, concentric ring lesions on older leaves. Favored by hot, dry weather followed by wet periods.
Potato Early blight.cure: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, azoxystrobin). Remove infected lower leaves.
Potato Early blight.prevention: Use certified seed potatoes, practice 3-year crop rotation, maintain plant vigor, apply preventive fungicides.

Potato Late blight.cause: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Large dark lesions on leaves with white mold on undersides. The pathogen that caused the Irish Potato Famine.
Potato Late blight.cure: Apply systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, cymoxanil) as soon as symptoms appear. Destroy severely infected crops.
Potato Late blight.prevention: Use certified seed potatoes, plant in well-drained soil, apply preventive fungicides in humid conditions.

Corn Cercospora leaf spot Gray leaf spot.cause: Caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. Long, rectangular, gray-to-tan lesions parallel to leaf veins. Favored by warm, humid conditions.
Corn Cercospora leaf spot Gray leaf spot.cure: Apply triazole or strobilurin fungicides. There are no curative options once severe.
Corn Cercospora leaf spot Gray leaf spot.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids, practice crop rotation, till infected residue, avoid dense planting.

Corn Common rust.cause: Caused by Puccinia sorghi. Small, oval, powdery brick-red pustules on both leaf surfaces.
Corn Common rust.cure: Apply fungicides (triazoles, strobilurins) at early infection. Most commercial hybrids have adequate resistance.
Corn Common rust.prevention: Plant rust-resistant hybrids, apply preventive fungicides in high-risk areas.

Corn Northern Leaf Blight.cause: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum. Long, tan, cigar-shaped lesions on leaves. Can cause significant yield loss.
Corn Northern Leaf Blight.cure: Apply fungicides (propiconazole, pyraclostrobin) at early stages. Remove severely infected leaves.
Corn Northern Leaf Blight.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids, practice crop rotation, till infected residue.

Grape Black rot.cause: Caused by Guignardia bidwellii fungus. Brown lesions on leaves and mummified black berries.
Grape Black rot.cure: Apply fungicides (myclobutanil, captan). Remove mummified berries and infected canes.
Grape Black rot.prevention: Prune for good air circulation, apply fungicides from bud break, remove all mummified fruit.

Grape Esca Black Measles.cause: A complex wood disease caused by several fungi (Phaeomoniella, Phaeoacremonium). Causes leaf scorch and internal wood streaking.
Grape Esca Black Measles.cure: No effective chemical cure. Prune affected wood. Apply wound sealants after pruning.
Grape Esca Black Measles.prevention: Prune during dry weather, use sanitized tools, apply wound protectants.

Grape Leaf blight Isariopsis Leaf Spot.cause: Caused by Pseudocercospora vitis. Angular dark brown lesions on leaves, primarily near veins.
Grape Leaf blight Isariopsis Leaf Spot.cure: Apply copper-based fungicides or dithiocarbamates. Remove infected leaves.
Grape Leaf blight Isariopsis Leaf Spot.prevention: Ensure good air circulation, apply preventive copper sprays, remove infected debris.

Cherry Powdery mildew.cause: Caused by Podosphaera clandestina fungus. White powdery coating on young leaves and shoots.
Cherry Powdery mildew.cure: Apply sulfur-based fungicides or triazoles. Remove and destroy infected tissue.
Cherry Powdery mildew.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, ensure air circulation, apply preventive sprays at bud break.

Peach Bacterial spot.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni bacteria. Water-soaked spots turning angular dark lesions on leaves.
Peach Bacterial spot.cure: Apply copper-based bactericides in spring. Remove infected twigs during dormant pruning.
Peach Bacterial spot.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, apply copper sprays during dormant and early-season periods.

Strawberry Leaf scorch.cause: Caused by Diplocarpon earlianum fungus. Small, dark purple spots that enlarge and cause marginal leaf browning.
Strawberry Leaf scorch.cure: Apply fungicides (captan, thiram). Remove and destroy infected leaves.
Strawberry Leaf scorch.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, use drip irrigation, maintain proper spacing for airflow.

Squash Powdery mildew.cause: Caused by Erysiphe cichoracearum or Sphaerotheca fuliginea. White powdery patches on leaf surfaces.
Squash Powdery mildew.cure: Apply sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, or triazole fungicides. Remove severely infected leaves.
Squash Powdery mildew.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, avoid water stress, ensure good air circulation.

Orange Haunglongbing Citrus greening.cause: Caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, spread by the Asian citrus psyllid. Causes mottled, yellowing leaves and misshapen, bitter fruit.
Orange Haunglongbing Citrus greening.cure: No cure. Infected trees should be removed. Control psyllid populations with insecticides.
Orange Haunglongbing Citrus greening.prevention: Use certified disease-free nursery stock, aggressively control psyllid populations, quarantine infected areas.

Pepper bell Bacterial spot.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria. Water-soaked spots with yellow halos on leaves and fruit.
Pepper bell Bacterial spot.cure: Apply copper-based bactericides. Remove infected plant material promptly.
Pepper bell Bacterial spot.prevention: Use certified seeds, rotate crops, avoid overhead irrigation, use resistant varieties.

Soybean Early blight.cause: Caused by various fungal pathogens under stress conditions. Brown lesions with yellow halos on leaves.
Soybean Early blight.cure: Apply appropriate fungicides. Ensure proper plant nutrition.
Soybean Early blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties, practice crop rotation, maintain proper plant spacing.
